essentials.heading: AZs vs Regions vs Edge
essentials.body: A **Region** is a geographic area with multiple isolated **Availability Zones** (each one or more discrete data centers with independent power/cooling/networking, connected by low-latency links). **Edge locations** (CloudFront, Route 53, Global Accelerator) serve content close to users but are not for running app tiers. **HA design rule**: spread resources across **at least 2 AZs in one Region** (multi-AZ) so a single-AZ failure is survivable. Multi-AZ = high availability within a Region; **multi-Region** = disaster recovery and lower global latency. AZs give synchronous, low-latency replication; cross-Region is asynchronous over the public-ish backbone.

essentials.heading: RTO vs RPO
essentials.body: **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long you can be **down** before recovery — drives how fast you must stand the workload back up. **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much **data loss** (measured in time) is acceptable — drives backup/replication frequency. Example: RPO 1h means you back up at least hourly; RTO 4h means recovery must complete within 4 hours. Lower RTO/RPO = more standby infrastructure and more frequent replication = higher cost. These two numbers are the primary inputs that pick a DR strategy.

essentials.heading: The 4 DR Strategies
essentials.body:
From cheapest/slowest to most expensive/fastest:
- **Backup & Restore** — only back up data; rebuild infra on disaster. Lowest cost, **highest RTO (hours)**, RPO = backup frequency.
- **Pilot Light** — core data replicated and a minimal always-on core (e.g. DB) in DR Region; app servers off until needed. Lower RTO than B&R (tens of min), moderate cost.
- **Warm Standby** — a **scaled-down but fully functional** copy always running in DR; scale up on failover. RTO minutes, higher cost.
- **Multi-Site Active/Active** — full production in 2+ Regions serving traffic simultaneously. **Near-zero RTO/RPO**, highest cost and complexity. Match the stated budget + RTO/RPO to the cheapest strategy that still meets the targets.

diagram.caption: The four DR strategies trade cost against RTO/RPO: Backup & Restore is cheapest but slowest; Multi-Site Active/Active is near-instant but most expensive.

links.label: Disaster recovery options in the cloud

essentials.heading: Decoupling & Statelessness
essentials.body: Resilient apps are **stateless** behind a load balancer so any instance can serve any request and failed instances are replaced transparently. Pattern: **ELB (ALB/NLB) + Auto Scaling Group spanning multiple AZs** with health checks; unhealthy instances are drained and replaced. **Externalize session state** to ElastiCache (Redis) or DynamoDB instead of local memory so a lost instance doesn't lose user sessions. **Decouple** components with SQS/SNS/EventBridge so a slow or failed consumer doesn't take down producers (buffering, retries, DLQs). Avoid sticky local disk — use S3/EFS for shared storage.

essentials.heading: Data Durability & Backups
essentials.body:
- **EBS snapshots** are incremental, stored in S3 (11 nines durability), and can be **copied cross-Region** and shared cross-account for DR.
- **AWS Backup** centralizes backup policies across EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, S3 etc. via **backup plans** with schedules, lifecycle (warm→cold), and **cross-Region / cross-account copy**.
- **S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR)** asynchronously replicates objects to a bucket in another Region (needs versioning on both ends) for low-RPO object DR; **SRR** replicates within a Region.
- Use **lifecycle + retention** to control cost; cross-Region copy protects against Region-wide failure.

essentials.heading: Database HA Recap
essentials.body:
- **RDS Multi-AZ** — synchronous standby replica in another AZ, automatic failover via DNS; HA within a Region, not read scaling. **Multi-AZ DB cluster** adds two readable standbys.
- **RDS Cross-Region Read Replicas** — async; promote for DR (manual, higher RPO).
- **Aurora** — storage spans 3 AZs / 6 copies automatically; **Aurora Global Database** = sub-second cross-Region replication, RPO ~1s, RTO ~1 min on promotion — strong for low-RTO/RPO multi-Region DR.
- **DynamoDB Global Tables** — multi-Region, multi-active, last-writer-wins replication; near-zero RTO/RPO for active/active. **PITR** gives 35-day continuous restore.

essentials.heading: Route 53 Failover & Health Checks
essentials.body:
Route 53 routing policies enable regional failover:
- **Failover routing** — a **primary** record and a **secondary** record; Route 53 **health checks** the primary endpoint and, if unhealthy, automatically returns the secondary (DR) endpoint. Classic active/passive DR DNS pattern.
- **Health checks** can monitor an endpoint, other health checks (calculated), or a CloudWatch alarm.
- **Multivalue / weighted / latency / geoproximity** support active/active and traffic shifting. Set a **low TTL** so clients fail over quickly. Combine with **ALB cross-zone** health and **Global Accelerator** for faster, IP-stable failover.

labs.title: AWS Backup plan with cross-region copy
labs.intro: Create a **backup vault** in the primary and DR Regions, then a **backup plan** that runs daily and **copies** recovery points cross-Region for DR. Assign resources by tag.
labs.pulumi:
import json
import pulumi
import pulumi_aws as aws

east = aws.Provider("east", region="us-east-1")
west = aws.Provider("west", region="us-west-2")

dr_vault = aws.backup.Vault(
    "dr-vault", name="dr-vault",
    opts=pulumi.ResourceOptions(provider=west),
)
primary_vault = aws.backup.Vault(
    "primary-vault", name="primary-vault",
    opts=pulumi.ResourceOptions(provider=east),
)

backup_role = aws.iam.Role(
    "backup-role",
    assume_role_policy=json.dumps({
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [{
            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {"Service": "backup.amazonaws.com"},
        }],
    }),
    managed_policy_arns=[
        "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSBackupServiceRolePolicyForBackup",
    ],
)

plan = aws.backup.Plan(
    "daily-with-dr-copy",
    name="daily-with-dr-copy",
    rules=[aws.backup.PlanRuleArgs(
        rule_name="daily",
        target_vault_name=primary_vault.name,
        schedule="cron(0 5 * * ? *)",
        start_window=60,
        lifecycle=aws.backup.PlanRuleLifecycleArgs(delete_after=30),
        copy_actions=[aws.backup.PlanRuleCopyActionArgs(
            destination_vault_arn=dr_vault.arn,
            lifecycle=aws.backup.PlanRuleCopyActionLifecycleArgs(delete_after=30),
        )],
    )],
    opts=pulumi.ResourceOptions(provider=east),
)

aws.backup.Selection(
    "tagged",
    plan_id=plan.id,
    iam_role_arn=backup_role.arn,
    selection_tags=[aws.backup.SelectionSelectionTagArgs(
        type="STRINGEQUALS", key="backup", value="daily",
    )],
    opts=pulumi.ResourceOptions(provider=east),
)
labs.note: **Cleanup**: delete the backup selection, then the plan, then any recovery points in both vaults (`aws backup delete-recovery-point`), then both vaults, and the IAM role. Vaults cannot be deleted while they hold recovery points. With Pulumi run `pulumi destroy`.

labs.title: Route 53 failover between primary and DR endpoints
labs.intro: Create a **health check** on the primary endpoint and two records in a hosted zone: a **PRIMARY** failover record and a **SECONDARY** record pointing to the DR endpoint. Route 53 serves the secondary only when the primary health check fails.
labs.cli:
# Health check against the primary endpoint
aws route53 create-health-check \
  --caller-reference hadr-$(date +%s) \
  --health-check-config '{
    "Type":"HTTPS","FullyQualifiedDomainName":"primary.example.com",
    "Port":443,"ResourcePath":"/health",
    "RequestInterval":30,"FailureThreshold":3
  }'

# Primary failover record (uses the health check id from above)
aws route53 change-resource-record-sets --hosted-zone-id Z123456ABCDEFG \
  --change-batch '{"Changes":[{"Action":"UPSERT","ResourceRecordSet":{
    "Name":"app.example.com","Type":"A","TTL":60,
    "SetIdentifier":"primary","Failover":"PRIMARY",
    "HealthCheckId":"<HC_ID>",
    "ResourceRecords":[{"Value":"203.0.113.10"}]}}]}'

# Secondary (DR) failover record
aws route53 change-resource-record-sets --hosted-zone-id Z123456ABCDEFG \
  --change-batch '{"Changes":[{"Action":"UPSERT","ResourceRecordSet":{
    "Name":"app.example.com","Type":"A","TTL":60,
    "SetIdentifier":"secondary","Failover":"SECONDARY",
    "ResourceRecords":[{"Value":"198.51.100.20"}]}}]}'
labs.pulumi:
import pulumi_aws as aws

zone_id = "Z123456ABCDEFG"

hc = aws.route53.HealthCheck(
    "primary-hc",
    type="HTTPS",
    fqdn="primary.example.com",
    port=443,
    resource_path="/health",
    request_interval=30,
    failure_threshold=3,
)

primary = aws.route53.Record(
    "app-primary",
    zone_id=zone_id,
    name="app.example.com",
    type="A",
    ttl=60,
    records=["203.0.113.10"],
    set_identifier="primary",
    health_check_id=hc.id,
    failover_routing_policies=[
        aws.route53.RecordFailoverRoutingPolicyArgs(type="PRIMARY"),
    ],
)

secondary = aws.route53.Record(
    "app-secondary",
    zone_id=zone_id,
    name="app.example.com",
    type="A",
    ttl=60,
    records=["198.51.100.20"],
    set_identifier="secondary",
    failover_routing_policies=[
        aws.route53.RecordFailoverRoutingPolicyArgs(type="SECONDARY"),
    ],
)
labs.note: **Cleanup**: delete both resource record sets (UPSERT -> DELETE with identical record bodies), then delete the health check. With Pulumi `pulumi destroy`. Records must be removed before the health check, and the hosted zone itself is left intact unless you created it for this lab.

questions.stem: A company needs a DR plan for a stateless web app. The business accepts up to 4 hours of downtime and up to 1 hour of data loss, and wants the **lowest possible cost**. Which strategy best fits?
options: Multi-Site Active/Active across two Regions
Warm Standby with a scaled-down stack always running in the DR Region
Pilot Light with the database replicated and app servers off
Backup & Restore using AWS Backup with hourly backups and cross-Region copy
questions.explanation: RTO 4h and RPO 1h are both generous, and cost is the priority, so **Backup & Restore** with hourly backups (meets RPO 1h) copied cross-Region is the cheapest option that still meets the targets — infra is rebuilt within the 4h RTO. Pilot Light and Warm Standby meet the targets too but cost more by keeping standby resources running. Multi-Site Active/Active is the most expensive and is overkill for a 4h RTO.

questions.stem: An order-processing platform requires an **RTO of a few minutes** and an **RPO of seconds**, and the budget allows always-on standby infrastructure but not full duplicate production. Which DR approach is most appropriate?
options: Warm Standby: a scaled-down but fully functional copy in the DR Region, scaled up on failover
Pilot Light with only the database replicated
Single-AZ deployment with frequent snapshots
Backup & Restore with nightly EBS snapshots
questions.explanation: A few-minute RTO with always-on (but not full) standby infrastructure is the textbook definition of **Warm Standby** — a reduced-capacity running stack that is scaled up at failover. Backup & Restore gives hours of RTO. Pilot Light keeps app servers off, so spin-up adds time and it has higher RTO than warm standby. Single-AZ has no DR at all. Active/Active would meet it but exceeds the stated budget.

questions.stem: A global SaaS app must serve users from two Regions simultaneously with **near-zero RTO and RPO** and automatic regional failover for its primary data store. Which combination meets this? (Choose two.)
options: DynamoDB Global Tables for multi-active, multi-Region data
Route 53 latency or failover routing with health checks across both Regions
RDS Single-AZ with manual snapshots copied cross-Region
A single EC2 instance with an Elastic IP in one Region
questions.explanation: **DynamoDB Global Tables** give multi-Region, multi-active replication with near-zero RTO/RPO, and **Route 53** (latency/failover routing + health checks) directs users to a healthy Region and fails over automatically. RDS Single-AZ with manual cross-Region snapshots has a high RPO/RTO and is not active/active. A single EC2 + EIP in one Region provides no multi-Region resilience at all.

questions.stem: A relational workload must stay available if a single Availability Zone fails, with **automatic failover** and **no application data loss**, but only needs to run in one Region. What should you use?
options: RDS Single-AZ with automated backups enabled
RDS with a cross-Region read replica that you promote manually
Two independent RDS instances and application-level dual writes
RDS Multi-AZ deployment
questions.explanation: **RDS Multi-AZ** maintains a synchronous standby in another AZ and fails over automatically via DNS with no data loss — exactly matching single-Region, AZ-fault tolerance with automatic failover. A cross-Region read replica is asynchronous (data loss possible) and requires manual promotion. App-level dual writes are complex and error-prone. Single-AZ with backups still requires a manual restore and incurs data loss back to the last backup.

questions.stem: A multi-Region application uses Aurora and needs a managed database that provides **cross-Region replication with RPO of about 1 second and recovery in about a minute** after promoting the secondary Region. Which feature fits?
options: Aurora Serverless v2 in one Region
Aurora single-Region cluster with Multi-AZ
Aurora Global Database
RDS Multi-AZ DB cluster
questions.explanation: **Aurora Global Database** replicates storage to secondary Regions with typical RPO ~1s and RTO ~1min when you promote a secondary — the intended low-RTO/RPO multi-Region pattern. A single-Region Aurora cluster (even Multi-AZ) and an RDS Multi-AZ DB cluster provide only intra-Region HA. Aurora Serverless v2 in one Region addresses scaling, not cross-Region DR.

questions.stem: After a deployment, users behind an ALB lose their login sessions whenever Auto Scaling replaces an instance. What change best improves resilience without changing the load balancer?
options: Disable Auto Scaling so instances are never replaced
Enable ALB sticky sessions and store sessions on each instance's local disk
Move all instances into a single Availability Zone
Externalize session state to ElastiCache for Redis (or DynamoDB) so any instance can serve any request
questions.explanation: Storing session state in a shared store like **ElastiCache (Redis) or DynamoDB** makes instances **stateless**, so a replaced instance does not lose sessions — the resilient pattern. Sticky sessions with local disk still loses state when that instance is terminated. Disabling Auto Scaling removes self-healing. Consolidating into one AZ destroys high availability.

questions.stem: A company keeps critical objects in an S3 bucket and must ensure they survive a complete Region outage with low RPO, while keeping a continuously updated copy in a second Region. What should they configure?
options: S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR) with versioning enabled on both buckets
EBS snapshots of the bucket copied cross-Region
S3 Same-Region Replication (SRR) to a second bucket
S3 Lifecycle rules transitioning objects to Glacier in the same Region
questions.explanation: **S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR)** asynchronously copies objects to a bucket in another Region (versioning required on source and destination), surviving a Region outage with low RPO. Lifecycle-to-Glacier stays in the same Region and gives no Region-failure protection. SRR replicates only within the same Region. S3 buckets are not backed by EBS, so EBS snapshots are irrelevant.

questions.stem: An active/passive setup has a primary endpoint in us-east-1 and a DR endpoint in us-west-2. The team wants DNS to **automatically** send users to the DR endpoint only when the primary is unhealthy, with fast cutover. What should they configure in Route 53?
options: Failover routing with a health check on the primary and a low record TTL
Geolocation routing mapping continents to each Region
Simple routing with both IPs in one record set
Weighted routing splitting 50/50 between the two endpoints
questions.explanation: **Failover routing** with a **health check** on the primary returns the secondary (DR) record only when the primary is unhealthy — exactly the active/passive automatic cutover; a **low TTL** makes clients pick up the change quickly. Weighted 50/50 sends half the traffic to DR even when primary is healthy. Simple routing returns all values with no health awareness. Geolocation routes by user location, not by health.
